title: Splitting Channels Into Groups
concept: Splitting 64 input channels into 2 groups of 32 means each group's filters only have 32 input channels to work with — never touching the other group's 32.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a grouped convolution, what happens to the input channels before any filtering starts?

options.text: They're split into separate, non-overlapping groups
They're all merged into a single channel first
Half of them are deleted at random

exercises.explain: Right — channels are partitioned into groups first, and each group stays walled off from the others. Let's put real numbers on that.

cards.title: Time for real numbers
cards.body: You've seen the idea in the abstract — groups, walls, independent filters. Now let's run it with numbers you'd actually see in a real CNN layer.

cards.title: Channels per group = total ÷ groups
cards.body: If a layer has 64 input channels and you choose G = 2 groups, each group gets 64 ÷ 2 = 32 channels. Group 1 owns channels 1-32. Group 2 owns channels 33-64. The channel count must divide evenly by the number of groups.

cards.title: Two mailrooms, one building
cards.body: Picture an office building with 64 mailboxes, split into two separate, walled-off mailrooms of 32 boxes each. Mailroom 1's staff sorts only its own 32 boxes — they physically cannot reach into Mailroom 2's boxes, and vice versa.

cards.title: 32 in, filters out
cards.body: With 64 channels split into 2 groups of 32, say you want 128 total output channels — 64 from each group. Every filter in Group 1 has a depth of exactly 32 (matching Group 1's channel count), not 64. Group 1 produces 64 output channels, Group 2 produces another 64, and stacking them gives the full 128-channel output.

cards.title: Filters don't secretly stay 64 deep
cards.body: It's easy to assume each filter still has the same depth as the original 64-channel input, just applied to a subset. It doesn't — a Group 1 filter's weight tensor is only 32 channels deep. It's physically incapable of reading Group 2's data, because it has no weights allocated for those channels at all.

cards.title: 64 channels, split down the middle
cards.body: Two groups, two walled-off filter sets:

left.text:
Own filters, 32 channels deep
Never sees channels 33-64
Produces its own outputs

right.text:
Own filters, 32 channels deep
Never sees channels 1-32
Produces its own outputs

exercises.prompt: A layer with 96 input channels uses 3 groups. How many channels does each group get?

options.text: 96 — every group still gets all channels

exercises.explain: Right — 96 channels split evenly across 3 groups gives 32 channels per group.

exercises.prompt: True or false: a filter in one group of a grouped convolution has the same channel-depth as the original, ungrouped input.
exercises.explain: False — each filter's depth shrinks to match only its own group's channel count, not the full original input.

exercises.sentence: 64 input channels split into 2 groups gives each group ___ channels to work with.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly describes splitting 64 channels into 4 groups?

options.text: Each group gets 16 channels, and its filters are only 16 channels deep
Each group still gets all 64 channels, just processed on a separate GPU
The 64 channels are randomly reshuffled between groups on every forward pass

exercises.explain: 64 ÷ 4 = 16 channels per group — and filter depth shrinks to match.

exercises.prompt: A CNN layer has 128 input channels. You split it into 8 groups for a grouped convolution. How many input channels does a single filter in any one group actually 'see'?

options.text: 128 — filters always see the whole input
1 — grouped convs always reduce to single channels

exercises.explain: Correct: 128 ÷ 8 = 16 channels per group, and that's exactly the depth of every filter inside that group. Next: what splitting channels like this actually saves you in compute.
